The use case for Arel: dynamic query builders where conditions are assembled programmatically based on user input — without Arel, string concatenation creates injection risks.

Rails choosing ERB over Haml or Slim as the default template engine favors familiarity over conciseness. UNSAID: Haml and Slim are more concise (50% fewer lines) but require learning new syntax for every developer. ERB is HTML with Ruby embedded — any HTML developer understands it immediately without a learning curve. The performance difference: ERB, Haml, and Slim all compile to similar Ruby code and are not significantly different in render time. The practical choice: ERB for teams with HTML designers; Haml/Slim for Ruby-only teams who find the verbosity distracting. Rails 7+ with ViewComponent and Phlex brings component thinking that makes template choice less significant for structured UI code.

Rails with dry-rb ecosystem (dry-validation, dry-monads, dry-types) provides functional programming patterns for complex business logic. UNSAID: dry-validation defines schema-validated input contracts with precise error messages. dry-monads provides Result monad (Success/Failure) for Railway-Oriented Programming — chain operations where any failure short-circuits the chain. The pattern: a transaction (dry-transaction) composes multiple steps, each returning Success or Failure; the first Failure stops execution and propagates the error. This replaces exception-based flow control with explicit error types. The trade-off: dry-rb adds significant learning curve and is more complex than service objects with early returns. Justified for: financial calculations, complex multi-step processes with precise error semantics.

FAILURE: Using raw SQL string literals without sanitize_sql creates SQL injection vectors even in experienced Rails apps. UNSAID: User.where(Arel.sql("name = '#{name}'")) is vulnerable — Arel.sql signals Rails to trust the string literally. The safe alternatives: User.where('name = ?', name) or User.where(name: name). In migrations and raw_connection.execute: always use parameterized queries or sanitize_sql_array. The dangerous pattern often appears in complex ORDER BY clauses: User.order(Arel.sql(params[:sort])) — a sort param of '(SELECT password FROM admins LIMIT 1)' is a SQL injection. Allowlist valid sort fields: VALID_SORTS = %w[name email created_at]; User.order(VALID_SORTS.include?(params[:sort]) ? params[:sort] : 'name').

Rails Zeitwerk autoloader convention maps file paths to constant names with strict rules. UNSAID: app/models/user.rb must define User; app/models/admin/user.rb must define Admin::User. Files not following this convention cause NameError. Concerns: app/models/concerns/trackable.rb defines Trackable (not Models::Concerns::Trackable) because concerns directories are collapse-mapped. Custom namespace mapping: config.autoload_paths += %W[#{root}/lib] adds lib to autoloading but lib/foo/bar.rb must define Foo::Bar. Inflections matter: app/models/html_parser.rb can define HTMLParser only with a custom inflection: config/initializers/inflections.rb. Zeitwerk provides Zeitwerk::Loader.new for explicit loader configuration in gems.

Rails with Sentry or AppSignal provides error tracking and performance monitoring essential for production visibility. UNSAID: Sentry's Ruby SDK auto-captures exceptions, adds breadcrumbs (SQL queries, Redis calls, Sidekiq jobs), and traces N+1 performance issues. set_user({ id: current_user.id }) contextualizes errors with user info. For Sidekiq: Sentry's Sidekiq integration captures job failures with full context. The performance tracing feature shows which endpoints and jobs are slow with distributed traces across background jobs. AppSignal is the Rails-native alternative with better Active Job integration and lower per-event pricing. The operational rule: no Rails app should go to production without error tracking — silent failures are unacceptable for production systems.

Rails has_many dependent: :destroy fires callbacks and loads each record into memory for deletion — use dependent: :delete_all for performance-critical bulk deletes. UNSAID: When a User with 10,000 comments is destroyed with has_many :comments, dependent: :destroy, Rails loads ALL 10,000 comments into Ruby objects and calls destroy on each, firing callbacks. This can take 30+ seconds and exhaust memory. dependent: :delete_all issues a single DELETE FROM comments WHERE user_id = ? — instant and no memory overhead. The choice: use destroy if comments have further cascading associations or callbacks that must run; use delete_all if comments are leaf nodes with no downstream dependencies. Database-level CASCADE DELETE (ON DELETE CASCADE in migration) is the most efficient option.

Rails test parallelism with parallelize(workers: :number_of_processors) dramatically reduces test suite runtime for large codebases. UNSAID: Rails 6+ supports parallel test execution using processes (fork) or threads. Process parallelism (default) forks the Rails process N times — each fork has its own database connection and clean state. The database setup: each worker gets its own database (test-0, test-1, etc.) created from the schema. The DatabaseCleaner gem strategy must match: truncation for process parallelism (not transaction, which only cleans within the process). Test startup cost: forking N processes adds ~10 seconds overhead but saves minutes on 5000+ test suites. CI: match workers to available CPU count (GitHub Actions: 2 vCPUs = 2 workers).

FAILURE: Using Rails flash for AJAX/Turbo requests without Turbo Stream flash handling loses flash messages. UNSAID: Flash messages rely on the redirect-after-POST pattern — flash is set, user is redirected, next request renders the flash. With Turbo, form submissions may respond with Turbo Stream updates instead of redirects. Flash set in a Turbo Stream response is stored in the session but never rendered unless the next full page load reads it. The fix: respond to Turbo Stream requests with a turbo_stream.append(:flash, partial: 'flash') that renders the flash into a designated DOM element. The turbo-rails gem provides helpers for this pattern. Always test flash behavior in Turbo-enabled forms.

Rails with the Retriable gem and retry logic for external API calls prevents transient failures from cascading into user-visible errors. UNSAID: retriable(on: [Net::ReadTimeout, Faraday::ConnectionFailed], tries: 3, base_interval: 1, multiplier: 2) wraps external calls with exponential backoff. For payment APIs: retry on network errors but NOT on 4xx validation errors (retrying invalid card data wastes API quota). The circuit breaker pattern (using the circuitbox gem) stops retrying when an external service is degraded: after N failures, open the circuit and fail fast for subsequent requests until the service recovers. This prevents a slow external API from backing up the entire Rails thread pool with waiting retries.

Ruby 3.x Ractors and Fibers provide concurrency primitives that Rails has not yet fully adopted, representing the direction of Ruby concurrency evolution. UNSAID: Ruby's GIL (Global VM Lock) prevents true thread parallelism for CPU-bound work. Ractors (Ruby 3.0+) achieve parallel execution by isolating shared state — each Ractor has its own object space. However, Rails's global state (AR connection pool, thread-local storage, class-level caches) makes Ractor adoption non-trivial. Fibers (used by the Async gem and Falcon web server) provide cooperative concurrency for I/O-bound work — handling thousands of concurrent HTTP connections per process. The Rails roadmap includes Ractor compatibility, but as of 2025, Puma + multiple processes remains the production recommendation.

Rails with Prosopite or Bullet gem in development automatically detects N+1 queries and eager loading opportunities. UNSAID: Bullet hooks into ActiveRecord query notifications and compares association access patterns to eager loading. It warns when: an association is loaded N times without eager loading; an eager loaded association is never used (unnecessary eager load adding to query size). Configuration: Bullet.alert = true, Bullet.rails_logger = true, Bullet.add_footer = true. Prosopite is a modern alternative with lower false-positive rate. These tools are essential during development but must be disabled or set to logging-only in production (performance overhead). The goal: zero N+1 warnings in CI before merge.

Rails with Heroku, Render, or Railway for deployment provides PaaS convenience vs Kamal for VPS control. UNSAID: Heroku's dyno model: web dynos handle HTTP, worker dynos handle background jobs, addon services provide PostgreSQL and Redis. Cost: $25-50/month for a basic production setup. Render.com provides similar PaaS with persistent disks and background workers at lower cost. Railway.app provides the simplest deploys with automatic service detection. Kamal on a VPS (Hetzner/DigitalOcean): $6-20/month for equivalent compute with full control. The breakeven: Heroku becomes more expensive than Kamal + VPS at around 3 dynos. The operational investment: Kamal requires understanding Docker and VPS management; PaaS abstracts this entirely. Choose based on team ops capability.

FAILURE: Not using database-level locks for inventory or seat reservation causes overselling. UNSAID: SELECT FOR UPDATE in PostgreSQL locks the selected rows until the end of the transaction: Product.lock.find(id).with_lock { product.decrement!(:stock) unless product.stock <= 0 }. Without this lock, two concurrent requests both read stock = 1, both check stock > 0, both decrement, resulting in stock = -1 (oversold). Optimistic locking (lock_version) is insufficient for inventory — it raises an error on conflict but doesn't prevent the double-read. Pessimistic locking (SELECT FOR UPDATE) is the correct pattern for inventory management, seat reservations, and any 'claim one resource' scenario.

Rails Action Mailer preview classes must live in test/mailers/previews/ and are only available in development. UNSAID: Mailer previews render emails with fake data without sending them, accessible at /rails/mailers. The preview class inherits from ActionMailer::Preview and defines methods that return mailer objects: def welcome_email; UserMailer.welcome_email(User.first); end. The URL /rails/mailers/user_mailer/welcome_email renders the email. Security constraint: previews call the mailer with real data from the database — in development this is fine; in production (where you might want to enable previews for QA), you must carefully control access (authentication + IP restriction). The User.first call in previews often fails in CI with empty databases — use build_stubbed or FactoryBot.build.

Rails with Flipper gem provides feature flags for gradual rollouts and A/B testing without code deploys. UNSAID: Flipper enables/disables features per user, group, percentage, or actor. Flipper.enable_percentage_of_actors(:new_checkout, 25) enables for 25% of users. Flipper.enable_actor(:dark_mode, current_user) enables for one specific user. The database adapter stores flag states in the app database (no separate infrastructure). The Flipper UI provides a web dashboard for non-technical stakeholders to manage flags. The deployment pattern: deploy code behind a feature flag disabled by default, gradually roll out to 5%, 25%, 50%, 100%, then remove the flag code. This eliminates the all-or-nothing risk of traditional deploys.

Rails respond_to and format blocks handle multi-format responses (HTML, JSON, CSV, PDF) from a single controller action. UNSAID: respond_to do |format|; format.html; format.json { render json: @user }; format.csv { send_data @users.to_csv }; end dispatches based on Accept header or file extension. The Mime::Type registration: Mime::Type.register 'application/pdf', :pdf. For complex multi-format responses, respond_with (from responders gem) reduces boilerplate. The gotcha: when writing API controllers that serve JSON, add format.json to all relevant actions — forgetting leaves a 406 Not Acceptable response for JSON clients. The convention: HTML controllers inherit from ApplicationController; JSON API controllers inherit from Api::BaseController with default format: :json.

FAILURE: Scheduling recurring jobs with cron without idempotency causes duplicate processing on cron failures and rescheduling. UNSAID: A cron-triggered job that runs at 9am to send daily digests will send twice if the cron fires twice (common after server restarts or time changes). The idempotency requirement: the job must check if today's digest was already sent: DigestJob.perform_if_not_sent(Date.current). Implementation: a digests table with a unique index on (user_id, date), or a Redis key digest:user_123:2025-01-01 with TTL. The pattern: upsert the digest record before sending — if the upsert succeeds (new record), send; if it fails with unique violation (already exists), skip. This makes the job safe to run multiple times.

Rails with the Turbo and ActionController::Live enables streaming server-sent events for live updates without WebSockets. UNSAID: ActionController::Live includes SSE support: response.headers['Content-Type'] = 'text/event-stream'; sse = SSE.new(response.stream, retry: 300); sse.write({message: 'update'}, event: 'notification'). The client: const es = new EventSource('/stream'); es.addEventListener('notification', ...). SSE is one-directional (server to client), simpler than WebSockets, and HTTP-based (works through proxies and load balancers without special config). For unidirectional real-time updates (notifications, progress bars, live counters): SSE. For bidirectional communication (chat, collaborative editing): WebSockets via Action Cable.

Rails 8's decision to include SQLite as a first-class production database option represents a paradigm shift from the 'always use PostgreSQL' orthodoxy. UNSAID: SQLite in production was long dismissed due to concurrency limitations. Rails 8 with WAL mode, proper pragma configuration, and Solid Queue on a separate SQLite database resolves the main concerns for most workloads. The Litestack gem provides further optimization. The practical implication: a Rails 8 app on a $6/month VPS with SQLite has lower operational complexity, zero latency database access (no network round-trip), and excellent read performance. The scale ceiling: SQLite handles single-server workloads well; when you need multiple database replicas or write throughput exceeding SQLite's serialized writes, migrate to PostgreSQL.

Rails with Searchjoy and Ahoy::Tracker provides search analytics to improve relevance and discover failed searches. UNSAID: Searchjoy logs search queries, result counts, and click-throughs in the database. Query analysis reveals: searches with zero results (improve content or add redirects); queries with high impressions but low clicks (poor relevance); trending search terms (inform content strategy). The integration with Meilisearch or Elasticsearch: when a search returns zero results, log it as a 'no_results' event and trigger alternative suggestions. The business value: search analytics directly improve conversion by revealing intent-content mismatches that wouldn't be apparent from page analytics alone.

Rails with RuboCop and RuboCop-Rails enforces style consistency and catches common Rails anti-patterns automatically. UNSAID: rubocop-rails adds Rails-specific cops: Rails/SaveBang warns when save without ! ignores return value; Rails/HasManyOrHasOneDependent warns when associations lack dependent option; Rails/TimeZone warns when Time.now is used instead of Time.current. The .rubocop.yml configuration: inherit_gem: { rubocop-rails-omakase: ['rubakase.yml'] } uses the Rails team's official configuration (the omakase gem). Pre-commit hooks run RuboCop and auto-fix safe offenses with --autocorrect. The philosophy: automated style enforcement eliminates style debates in code review, freeing reviewers to focus on logic and design.

FAILURE: Rails config.force_ssl in production without proper SSL termination causes infinite redirects. UNSAID: config.force_ssl = true redirects all HTTP requests to HTTPS. If SSL is terminated at the load balancer (common with Nginx, Heroku, AWS ALB) and the Rails app receives plain HTTP from the load balancer, Rails sees HTTP and redirects to HTTPS indefinitely. The fix: trust the X-Forwarded-Proto header from the load balancer: config.force_ssl = true AND ensure the load balancer sets X-Forwarded-Proto: https. With Thruster (Rails 8's built-in proxy), SSL termination happens at Thruster which handles this correctly. For Nginx: proxy_set_header X-Forwarded-Proto https must be in the upstream config.

Rails with Devise and OmniAuth enables social authentication (Google, GitHub, Apple) alongside email/password. UNSAID: OmniAuth strategies handle the OAuth handshake; Devise's omniauthable module connects OmniAuth callbacks to Devise's session management. The callback controller pattern: find_or_create_user_from_oauth(auth_hash) handles both new users (create) and existing users (find by provider + uid). The common gotcha: OmniAuth 2.0+ requires CSRF protection for GET-based OAuth initiation — routes.rb needs get '/users/auth/:provider', to: 'users/omniauth_callbacks#passthru'. Apple Sign In has additional complexity: user data (name, email) is only sent once on first sign-in — store it immediately or lose it permanently.

Rails database connection pool sizing requires matching web server concurrency to avoid connection exhaustion. UNSAID: Puma with 5 threads per worker × 2 workers = 10 concurrent database connections maximum. database.yml pool: 5 per worker = 10 total connections. If pool < threads, threads wait for connections (ConnectionTimeoutError). If pool > database max_connections, the database refuses connections. PostgreSQL default max_connections = 100 — shared across all app instances. With 3 Puma instances × 5 threads: pool must not exceed 33 connections total. PgBouncer pools connections at the proxy level: all Rails processes share a smaller PostgreSQL connection pool. For serverless/many-process deployments: always use PgBouncer or Prisma Accelerate equivalent.

FAILURE: Not adding indexes for foreign keys slows JOIN queries significantly on large tables. UNSAID: When Rails generates a belongs_to association, it creates the foreign key column but NOT the index by default (Rails 5+: add_reference enables the index: true option but teams forget). A query like Post.joins(:comments).where(comments: { published: true }) scans the entire comments table to find matching post_id values without an index on comments.post_id. Add indexes: add_index :comments, :post_id in migrations. The rule: every foreign key column needs a database index. The audit: User.connection.tables.each { |t| check_fk_indexes(t) }. Missing FK indexes are a top-5 performance problem in Rails apps with real data.

Rails with ActionPolicy provides a framework for authorization policies with first-class testing and scoping support. UNSAID: action_policy is an alternative to Pundit with more Rails integration: it auto-infers policy classes, provides policy_for helper, and includes explicit pre-check support (before-authorization hooks). The scoped_to helper in policies returns ActiveRecord scopes: def index?; true; end; scope_matcher :active_record_relation, ActionPolicy::Scope::ActiveRecordRelation; def scope_for(relation); user.admin? ? relation : relation.where(user: user); end. Compared to Pundit: ActionPolicy has better Rails integration but is less widely adopted. For apps starting fresh: both are excellent; for apps with existing Pundit: stick with it.

Rails with the Discard gem provides soft deletion — marking records as deleted without removing them from the database. UNSAID: discard gem adds discarded_at timestamp to models. record.discard sets discarded_at = now; record.undiscard clears it. The default scope excludes discarded records: Model.kept returns only active records. Discard is preferred over acts_as_paranoid (which patches destroy and complicates AR behavior). The use cases: audit trails requiring historical data; deleted record recovery; soft-deleted records referenced by other records that should remain valid. The constraint: soft-deleted records still consume storage and index space — periodic hard purging of old soft-deleted records (> 90 days) is necessary for large tables.

FAILURE: Using Rails secrets.yml (deprecated) instead of credentials.yml.enc in Rails 5.2+ exposes secrets in plaintext. UNSAID: Rails 5.1 and earlier used config/secrets.yml which was often committed to version control with development/test secrets, occasionally including production credentials. Rails 5.2 introduced encrypted credentials (credentials.yml.enc) which is safe to commit. The migration: move all secrets to credentials.yml.enc using rails credentials:edit; remove secrets.yml; update references from Rails.application.secrets.key to Rails.application.credentials.key. The security audit: check git history for credentials.yml or secrets.yml commits — once committed, the secret is compromised and must be rotated even if the file is deleted.

Rails with Noticed and Action Cable provides a complete notification system with real-time delivery and read/unread tracking. UNSAID: The complete pattern: Noticed stores notifications in a database table (via DeliveryMethod::Database); Action Cable broadcasts a Turbo Stream that appends the notification to the bell icon counter and dropdown in real-time; the notification model has a read_at timestamp for tracking; marking as read updates read_at and morphs the notification element to show read state. The bell icon shows unread count via a counter cache on the User model. This pattern implements the full GitHub-style notification experience without any client-side JavaScript beyond the Action Cable subscription.

Rails with Turbo requires CSRF token handling for non-GET requests, which changes behavior compared to traditional AJAX. UNSAID: Turbo automatically includes the CSRF token in all form submissions. For fetch() calls from Stimulus controllers: include the token manually from document.querySelector('[name=csrf-token]')?.content as the X-CSRF-Token header. The gotcha: API endpoints that accept JSON with format.json { } still require CSRF tokens unless the controller includes protect_from_forgery except: :json (for true API controllers). Forgetting CSRF for custom fetch calls results in 422 Unprocessable Entity responses. The Rails default: protect_from_forgery with: :exception — always active for non-API controllers.

Rails code quality with SimpleCov measures test coverage and enforces coverage thresholds in CI. UNSAID: SimpleCov generates HTML coverage reports showing which lines are tested. Configuration: SimpleCov.minimum_coverage 80 fails CI if coverage drops below 80%. Branch coverage (SimpleCov.enable_coverage :branch) measures if-else branch coverage, not just line coverage. The practical workflow: run tests, view report, identify untested branches, add tests. Coverage theater: 100% line coverage with no assertion testing is possible — coverage is a necessary but not sufficient indicator of test quality. Combine SimpleCov with mutation testing (mutant gem) for higher confidence: mutant changes your code and verifies tests catch the changes.

Rails with Lookbook provides component previews and documentation for ViewComponent and Phlex components during development. UNSAID: Lookbook mounts at /rails/lookbook in development and provides a browsable catalog of all ViewComponent previews with live parameter editing. The annotation system: add @label, @param, and @note annotations to preview methods to document component APIs. Lookbook replaces Storybook for Rails-native component development — it uses the same preview files as ViewComponent's built-in previews but adds a richer UI. For design systems: Lookbook serves as both development tool and living documentation. The combination: ViewComponent + Lookbook + RSpec component tests creates a robust component development workflow.

FAILURE: Mutating frozen string literals in Ruby causes RuntimeError: can't modify frozen String. UNSAID: Ruby 3.x defaults (and Rails apps that enable # frozen_string_literal: true) freeze all string literals. String operations that mutate: str << 'appended', str.replace('new'), str[0] = 'x', str.upcase! all raise RuntimeError on frozen strings. The fix: use non-mutating methods (str + 'appended', str.upcase) or .dup to get a mutable copy. The performance benefit: frozen strings are deduplicated in memory — identical frozen strings share the same object. The migration path: run bundle exec rubocop --auto-correct to find and fix mutable string operations before enabling frozen string literals globally.

Rails with Tailwind CSS component patterns using custom CSS layers provides the missing design system layer. UNSAID: Tailwind's @layer components enables defining component classes: @layer components { .btn { @apply px-4 py-2 rounded font-medium; } }. This avoids repetitive utility class strings in ERB templates while keeping Tailwind's purging benefits. The pattern: base layer for elements, components layer for reusable UI primitives (btn, card, badge), utilities for one-off adjustments. ViewComponent + Tailwind components: the ViewComponent handles Ruby logic, the template uses component CSS classes. The critical rule: component CSS classes in @layer components are purged if not found in template scan paths — configure Tailwind content to include all .html.erb and .rb files.

Ruby on Rails 8's full-stack vision in 2025 positions it as the best framework for a single developer building a production web application. UNSAID: The Rails 8 stack: authentication generator (no Devise learning curve), Solid trifecta (no Redis), Kamal 2 (no PaaS), Thruster (no Nginx config), Propshaft + importmap (no Node.js), Tailwind (via standalone binary), Turbo + Stimulus (no React). A Rails 8 developer can go from idea to production on a $6/month VPS in a single day. The trade-offs: this stack is optimized for productivity and simplicity, not for teams needing React's component ecosystem, GraphQL, or horizontal database scaling. For 99% of SaaS apps with < 100k users, Rails 8 stack is technically sufficient and operationally simpler than alternatives.

Rails with the propshaft and jsbundling-rails combination handles modern JavaScript (TypeScript, npm packages) without sacrificing Propshaft's simplicity. UNSAID: jsbundling-rails integrates esbuild, rollup, or webpack as the JavaScript bundler. esbuild is recommended: 100x faster than webpack, TypeScript support built-in, zero config for most use cases. The setup: app/javascript/application.js is the entry point; esbuild bundles to app/assets/builds/application.js; Propshaft serves the built file with digest fingerprinting. npm packages (via package.json) are bundled by esbuild. The distinction from vite_rails: jsbundling-rails is simpler but lacks Vite's HMR and plugin ecosystem; vite_rails is better for apps heavily using modern JS frameworks.

Rails Active Job serializes job arguments to JSON, which means non-serializable objects (Ruby objects, Procs, complex class instances) cannot be passed as job arguments. UNSAID: ActiveJob::Arguments.serialize converts arguments to JSON-safe primitives. Supported: strings, numbers, arrays, hashes, booleans, nil, and GlobalID-serializable objects (ActiveRecord models). Not supported: arbitrary Ruby objects, Symbols (serialized as strings in some adapters), Procs/lambdas, Time objects with timezone info (use ISO8601 strings). The pattern: always pass IDs, not objects: SomeJob.perform_later(user.id) not SomeJob.perform_later(user). When the job executes, re-fetch the record: user = User.find(user_id). This handles the case where the record changes between enqueue and execution.

FAILURE: Deploying Rails without asset precompilation causes 500 errors on pages loading assets. UNSAID: In development, assets are compiled on-the-fly. In production, RAILS_ENV=production rails assets:precompile must run before the app starts. Kamal handles this in the Dockerfile build step. For Heroku: the buildpack runs assets:precompile automatically. For manual deploys: forgetting this step causes ActionView::Template::Error: asset not precompiled. The Dockerfile pattern: RUN bundle exec rails assets:precompile during image build (with a dummy SECRET_KEY_BASE env var since the real one is only available at runtime). The Propshaft equivalent: assets:precompile copies and fingerprints assets to public/assets/.

Rails with Pagy and Turbo Frames provides infinite scroll or paginated navigation without JavaScript. UNSAID: The Turbo Frame pattern for pagination: wrap the post list in a turbo-frame; the 'next page' link targets the same frame. Clicking loads the next page, replacing the frame content. For infinite scroll: add the next-page link in a div, use Stimulus with IntersectionObserver to click the link when it enters viewport. Pagy provides pagy_next_url for the next page URL. The Turbo Morphing approach (Rails 8): broadcasts page refresh on scroll to bottom, morphing in new content without full reload. Pagy vs Kaminari: Pagy has 40x lower memory overhead for large page counts.

Rails OpenAPI documentation with rswag or jsonapi-serializer documentation provides API consumer contracts. UNSAID: rswag generates OpenAPI (Swagger) specs from RSpec integration tests — the tests ARE the documentation. spec/swagger/ contains the spec definitions; rswag:specs:swaggerize generates swagger.json from the tests. The Swagger UI is mounted at /api-docs. This approach ensures docs stay synchronized with the actual API behavior (failing test = inaccurate doc). Alternative: API Blueprint + Dredd tests the API against documentation. For Rails API apps exposed to third parties, OpenAPI docs reduce integration support burden significantly.

FAILURE: Using ActiveRecord callbacks to enqueue background jobs causes jobs to enqueue before transaction commits. UNSAID: after_create { SendWelcomeEmailJob.perform_later(self) } inside a transaction context enqueues the job immediately when the callback runs — before the outer transaction commits. If the transaction rolls back (another operation fails), the job runs against a non-existent record. The fix: use after_commit callbacks instead of after_create/after_save: after_commit :enqueue_welcome_email, on: :create. after_commit fires ONLY after the transaction commits to the database. Alternatively, use the after_commit_everywhere gem to use after_commit semantics anywhere in your app code. This is the most common source of 'record not found' errors in background jobs.

Rails choosing ActiveRecord over DataMapper as the ORM approach reflects a pragmatic over purity choice with long-term maintenance implications. UNSAID: ActiveRecord ties models directly to database tables (Active Record pattern) — a model IS its database representation. DataMapper (ROM, Sequel) separates the database model from the domain model — a database row maps TO a domain object. ActiveRecord wins in Rails apps because it's the convention: fewer files, less mapping code, immediate CRUD operations. DataMapper wins for complex domains where the database schema shouldn't dictate domain object design. As Rails apps grow complex: the boundary between database model and domain model becomes limiting. Service objects, form objects, and domain objects compensate for ActiveRecord's coupling in well-architected Rails apps.

Rails with ActiveSupport::Notifications provides an instrumentation API for measuring and monitoring application events. UNSAID: ActiveSupport::Notifications.instrument('my.event', { key: value }) publishes events. Subscribers: ActiveSupport::Notifications.subscribe('my.event') { |name, start, finish, id, payload| log_it }. Rails uses this internally for sql.active_record, render_template.action_view, process_action.action_controller events. Custom instrumentation enables business-level metrics: instrument('payment.processed', amount: order.total, user: user.id) published to the notification system, consumed by a metrics subscriber that sends to StatsD/Prometheus. This decouples instrumentation from metric delivery — change the metric backend without changing application code.

Rails with the Marginalia gem annotates SQL queries with the controller, action, and job name for debugging slow queries. UNSAID: Marginalia appends SQL comments to every query: /* controller=users,action=index */ SELECT * FROM users. When reading PostgreSQL's pg_stat_activity or slow query log, these annotations immediately show which part of the app generated the query. Essential for: debugging which endpoint causes a slow query, attribution in multi-tenant apps, and correlation between application errors and database slowdowns. Marginalia integrates with Rails query logs (config.active_record.query_log_tags) in Rails 7+, making it the standard approach for query attribution. Zero performance overhead — SQL comments are stripped by the database before execution.

FAILURE: Embedding user-controlled content in SQL LIKE patterns without escaping creates SQL injection via wildcard abuse. UNSAID: User.where('name LIKE ?', "%#{params[:name]}%") looks safe (parameterized query) but if params[:name] contains % or _, those are SQL wildcard characters. A user searching for '%' matches all names; '___' matches 3-char names. This is not a full SQL injection but allows unauthorized data enumeration. The fix: sanitize_sql_like(params[:name]) before embedding in LIKE patterns. User.where('name LIKE ?', "%#{User.sanitize_sql_like(params[:name])}%") escapes %, _, and \ properly. This is a frequently missed security issue because the query uses parameterization and looks 'safe'.

Rails with the Brakeman static analysis tool automatically identifies security vulnerabilities without running the application. UNSAID: Brakeman scans Rails source code for: SQL injection, XSS, mass assignment, CSRF, unprotected redirects, dangerous deserialization (YAML.load with user input), and insecure configurations. It produces a report with severity levels and the specific vulnerable code locations. Running in CI: brakeman -q --no-pager exits with non-zero status if high-confidence vulnerabilities are found. The integration: brakeman --format json outputs machine-readable results for SIEM integration. False positive management: brakeman.ignore file tracks acknowledged false positives. Every Rails app should run Brakeman in CI — it catches vulnerabilities that code review misses.

Rails model validations run before save but NOT on update_column, update_all, or direct SQL updates. UNSAID: update_column(:status, 'invalid_status') skips validations and callbacks, writing directly to the database. This is intentional for performance-critical updates that don't need validation (e.g., updating timestamps, incrementing counters). The danger: using update_column for data that requires validation creates invalid database state. Rule: use update_column only for attributes where you've already verified validity or for system-managed fields. update(key: value) runs validations and callbacks; update_column(key, value) does not. Also: update_all { |r| } skips all validations, callbacks, and timestamps for bulk updates.

Rails with Papaparse or streaming CSV generation handles large CSV exports without memory exhaustion. UNSAID: Building a 100k-row CSV with CSV.generate { } accumulates all rows in memory before sending. The streaming approach: response.headers['Content-Type'] = 'text/csv'; response.headers['Content-Disposition'] = 'attachment; filename=export.csv'; self.response_body = Enumerator.new { |y| y << CSV.generate_line(headers); User.find_each { |u| y << CSV.generate_line([u.id, u.email]) } }. This streams rows to the client as they're generated, using constant memory regardless of dataset size. For async exports: enqueue a background job that writes to Active Storage and emails the download link — more reliable for very large exports that take minutes.

FAILURE: Not verifying Stripe webhook signatures allows spoofed webhook events that manipulate order or subscription state. UNSAID: Stripe signs every webhook payload with a secret (Stripe-Signature header). Not verifying: any POST to your webhook endpoint with a fake order_completed event changes subscription state. Verification: Stripe::Webhook.construct_event(request.body.read, request.env['HTTP_STRIPE_SIGNATURE'], webhook_secret) raises an error if the signature is invalid. The gotcha: you must read request.body.read before any middleware processes the body (JSON parsing changes the body). The route must skip CSRF verification (skip_before_action :verify_authenticity_token) since Stripe doesn't send CSRF tokens. Always verify webhook signatures for all payment providers.

Rails with database-level generated columns and virtual attributes enables computed fields without application-level callbacks. UNSAID: PostgreSQL generated columns: full_name GENERATED ALWAYS AS (first_name || ' ' || last_name) STORED stores the computed value; VIRTUAL computes on read. Rails migration: add_column :users, :full_name, :virtual, type: :string, as: 'first_name || last_name', stored: true. Generated columns are indexed, searchable, and always consistent — no callback timing issues. The use case: search vectors (full-text tsvector generated from multiple text columns), formatted identifiers, and denormalized values for performance. Requires PostgreSQL 12+. The Rails virtual attribute via calculated: User.select('*, first_name || last_name as full_name') provides similar without schema changes.

Rails with ActiveRecord's upsert and upsert_all enables efficient insert-or-update operations for bulk data synchronization. UNSAID: User.upsert({ email: 'a@b.com', name: 'Alice' }, unique_by: :email) inserts or updates in a single SQL statement (INSERT ... ON CONFLICT DO UPDATE). upsert_all processes an array of hashes in one query. The performance: inserting 1000 records with upsert_all: 1 query vs 1000 queries with find_or_create_by. The constraint: upsert skips callbacks and validations — use for data sync from trusted sources, not user input. The conflict_target option specifies which columns define uniqueness; the returning option specifies which columns to return from the upserted rows.

Rails with Turbo's data-turbo-confirm and data-turbo-method attributes enables declarative confirmation dialogs and non-GET methods without JavaScript. UNSAID: data-turbo-method='delete' on a link submits a DELETE request via Turbo (sends a hidden form under the hood). data-turbo-confirm='Are you sure?' shows a browser confirm dialog before the request. For custom confirmation dialogs: override Turbo.config.forms.confirm with a Promise-returning function. These HTML attributes replace the old data-method and data-confirm from Rails UJS (which required jquery_ujs). The migration from UJS to Turbo: remove jquery_ujs; ensure all links with data-method now use data-turbo-method; the behavior is identical but Turbo-native.

Rails ActiveRecord's destroy_all on a relation without conditions destroys all records in the table. UNSAID: The terrifying no-condition destroy: User.destroy_all deletes every user. User.where(role: 'guest').destroy_all is safe. The safeguard: add a check in the model or use a dry-run mode. In Rails console, always: User.where(condition).count before User.where(condition).destroy_all. For data migrations: never run in the console without reviewing the WHERE clause. The database-level protection: backups before any bulk destroy, point-in-time recovery enabled. Monitoring: before a bulk destroy, log the count and condition; after, verify the count difference.

Rails with the Postmark gem provides transactional email delivery with delivery tracking and bounce handling. UNSAID: Postmark's Rails integration: config.action_mailer.delivery_method = :postmark, postmark: { api_token: credentials.postmark_token }. Postmark tracks opens, clicks, bounces, and spam complaints via webhooks. The webhook handler updates email delivery status in the database. Bounce handling: suppress re-sending to bounced addresses using Postmark's suppression list or maintaining a local suppression list updated by webhooks. Message streams: transactional emails and bulk emails use separate streams with separate IP pools — crucial for maintaining transactional deliverability when bulk emails trigger spam filters.

FAILURE: Rails app initialization code in config/initializers runs during every request in development mode. UNSAID: In development with class reloading enabled, Rails reloads all application code between requests but initializers only run at startup. If an initializer reads a constant (like a model) and stores it, the stored reference becomes stale after reload. The pattern that breaks: MY_CONSTANT = User.all in an initializer — User class is reloaded but MY_CONSTANT still holds the old relation. Fix: lazy evaluation — define a method or proc that evaluates at use time: MY_THING = -> { User.active }; MY_THING.call. For configuration that must be evaluated at startup: use config.after_initialize in config/application.rb.

Rails with Bullet + RSpec + DatabaseCleaner provides the complete test isolation and performance monitoring setup. UNSAID: DatabaseCleaner strategy: transaction for most tests (fast, wraps test in a transaction that rolls back); truncation for tests using multiple database connections or Capybara system tests (transaction rollback doesn't work across connections). RSpec tag-based strategy switching: :js tests use truncation; other tests use transaction. Bullet in spec: Bullet.start; around(:each) { Bullet.start_request; example.run; Bullet.end_request }. Violations reported as test failures: Bullet.raise (optional but strictest). This combination catches N+1s during the test run, not just in development.

Rails Railtie enables gems to integrate deeply with the Rails lifecycle without requiring explicit user setup. UNSAID: A Railtie hooks into: config (add default config values), initializers (run code at startup), rake tasks (add new rake tasks), generators (add scaffold generators), and middleware (insert Rack middleware). The pattern for a gem: class MyGem::Railtie < Rails::Railtie; initializer 'my_gem.configure_rails_initialization' { MyGem.setup }; end. This is how gems like Devise, Pundit, and Sidekiq integrate: requiring the gem in the Gemfile is sufficient — no manual setup code needed in config/. Understanding Railties helps debug gem initialization order issues.

FAILURE: Using render :json without specifying status code returns 200 for errors, breaking API clients. UNSAID: render json: { error: 'Not found' } returns HTTP 200 with an error body — clients checking status code assume success. The correct pattern: render json: { error: 'Not found' }, status: :not_found or render json: { error: 'Unauthorized' }, status: :unauthorized. Rails provides symbol status helpers: :ok (200), :created (201), :no_content (204), :bad_request (400), :unauthorized (401), :forbidden (403), :not_found (404), :unprocessable_entity (422), :internal_server_error (500). API design rule: the HTTP status code conveys the response type; the body provides details. Never return 200 for error responses.

Ruby's expressiveness and Rails' magic enable rapid prototyping but create maintenance challenges as teams grow. UNSAID: The productivity advantage of Rails is real: a Rails developer builds features 2-3x faster than equivalent Java/Spring or Go/Gin due to conventions, generators, and AR magic. The maintenance challenge emerges when: multiple developers have different mental models of the 'magic'; implicit behavior makes debugging non-obvious; and 'Rails magic' diverges from expected behavior due to version upgrades. The mitigation: explicit over implicit where readability is at stake; thorough test coverage to document behavior; upgrade Rails regularly (one major version behind is acceptable; two is accumulating debt). The community insight: Rails apps that survive 10 years are maintained by teams who understand and embrace the conventions.

Rails with Solid Queue and recurring tasks provides cron-like scheduling without external cron or Sidekiq Enterprise. UNSAID: Solid Queue's RecurringTask DSL schedules jobs: config.solid_queue.recurring_tasks = { daily_digest: { class: 'DailyDigestJob', schedule: 'every day at 9am' } }. The schedule is parsed by the Fugit gem (cron syntax and natural language). Tasks are registered in the database and executed by Solid Queue's scheduler process. The advantage: recurring tasks survive server restarts (state in the database), don't require an external cron daemon, and appear in Mission Control Jobs UI. For Sidekiq: Sidekiq-Cron or Sidekiq-Scheduler gems provide equivalent functionality backed by Redis.

Rails with the ActiveRecord Enum provides state machine-like behavior for columns with a fixed set of valid values. UNSAID: enum status: { draft: 0, published: 1, archived: 2 } on Post creates: post.draft!, post.published?, Post.published scope, post.status_before_type_cast. The integer storage is efficient; string storage (enum status: { draft: 'draft' }) is more debuggable in raw database queries. The validation: enum validates that only defined values are set; invalid values raise ArgumentError immediately. The pitfall: reordering enum values changes integer mapping for existing data. Never reorder — only add new values at the end. For complex state machines with transitions and guards: use the AASM gem which adds transition validation and callbacks.

FAILURE: Storing serialized Ruby objects in the database with YAML.load creates remote code execution vulnerabilities. UNSAID: serialize :data in Rails 4 used YAML.load which deserializes arbitrary Ruby objects. An attacker who controls the serialized data can execute arbitrary code through YAML deserialization gadget chains. Rails 7.1 changed the default serializer to JSON (which can't deserialize arbitrary objects). If upgrading from YAML serialization: migrate existing data to JSON, then change the coder. For any column that stores user-controlled data: never use YAML.load (always YAML.safe_load); prefer JSON. The Brakeman scanner detects dangerous deserializations. This vulnerability class (deserialization gadget chains) is a critical OWASP risk.

Rails with PgHero provides a database monitoring dashboard for PostgreSQL query performance, index usage, and connection health. UNSAID: PgHero mounts at /pghero (protected by authentication) and shows: slow queries with EXPLAIN output, unused indexes wasting storage, duplicate indexes, missing indexes suggested by pg_stat_user_tables, connection pool status, and table bloat from dead tuples. The query stats feature: pg_stat_statements extension must be enabled in PostgreSQL. PgHero's autovacuum monitoring shows tables that need manual VACUUM ANALYZE. The suggested indexes feature analyzes queries and recommends missing indexes automatically. Essential for production PostgreSQL monitoring without setting up separate monitoring infrastructure.

Rails Active Storage's public: true option stores files without signed URLs, making them directly accessible via CDN but losing access control. UNSAID: By default, Active Storage uses signed URLs (expiring, authenticated) — perfect for user-specific documents. public: true stores files in a public bucket location, accessible without authentication — appropriate for: product images, avatars, any public media. The design decision: if a file should be accessible without logging in, use public: true and serve via CDN for performance. If the file is user-specific (invoices, medical records), use private storage with signed URLs. The S3 bucket policy must match: public files need a public-read bucket policy; private files need no public access. Mixing private and public files in the same bucket requires careful configuration.

Rails health check endpoint and readiness probe integration with Kubernetes or Docker health checks ensures only healthy instances receive traffic. UNSAID: Rails 7.1+ includes /up as a built-in health check endpoint returning 200 if the app can serve requests. Custom health checks: extend the route to verify database connectivity and Redis availability: def check; ActiveRecord::Base.connection.execute('SELECT 1'); head :ok; rescue => e; head :service_unavailable; end. Kubernetes liveness probe: checks if the process is alive (restart if dead). Readiness probe: checks if the app can serve traffic (remove from load balancer if database is unavailable). The distinction: a liveness failure restarts the pod; a readiness failure removes it from the service endpoint temporarily.

FAILURE: Calling render or redirect_to without return in a before_action allows the controller action to continue executing after the response is started. UNSAID: def check_auth; redirect_to login_path unless current_user; end — if the redirect runs, Rails starts sending the response, but the action (def show) ALSO runs, causing double render errors. The fix: def check_auth; redirect_to(login_path) and return unless current_user; end. The same issue in actions: if @user.nil?; render :not_found; end and the code below continues. Best practice: use redirect_to ... and return or if/else/end structures where only one branch renders. The before_action with return is cleaner: redirect_to(login_path) and return unless current_user — the and return pattern is idiomatic Rails.

Rails with PaperTrail and the revert functionality enables undo operations for user-facing data changes. UNSAID: PaperTrail's version.reify returns an ActiveRecord object in its previous state — calling save on it reverts the record. The UI pattern: store version.id in the success flash message; show an 'Undo' link that sends the version_id to a revert action; the action loads the version and calls reify.save!. Time-windowed undo: only show the undo link for 5 minutes using the version's created_at timestamp. The gotcha: reverting a delete requires checking if dependent records were also deleted (they have their own versions). Full system undo of complex operations requires reverting all related versions in the correct order.

Rails GraphQL subscriptions with ActionCable enable real-time GraphQL data push to connected clients. UNSAID: GraphQL Ruby + ActionCable provides subscription support: subscription fields define what triggers an update; clients subscribe via WebSocket; server pushes updates when subscribed data changes. The trigger: GraphQL::Schema.subscriptions.trigger('orderUpdated', { order_id: order.id }, order) broadcasts to all subscribed clients. The channel: graphql-ws or graphql-subscriptions-client handles the WebSocket protocol on the frontend. The practical pattern: combine GraphQL queries/mutations (HTTP) with subscriptions (WebSocket) for efficient real-time apps that avoid polling. The operational consideration: each subscribed client holds an ActionCable connection — plan for connection scaling.

Rails cookie store session has a 4KB hard limit — exceeding it silently truncates or fails to set the cookie. UNSAID: The CookieStore serializes and encrypts the entire session hash into the cookie value. 4KB includes the encryption overhead (~30%) and base64 encoding (~33%). Effective data limit: ~2.5KB. Storing complex objects or arrays in session approaches this limit quickly. Symptom of overflow: session data is lost silently on some requests, users get logged out randomly, or authentication failures on pages with large session data. The diagnostic: log session.to_yaml.bytesize in ApplicationController before_action. Solution: switch to server-side session storage (Redis, database) using config.session_store :redis_store or :active_record_store.

Rails with the Whenever gem or cron: config in Kamal provides declarative cron job scheduling for production. UNSAID: Whenever translates a Ruby DSL to crontab entries: every 1.day, at: '9am' { rake 'digests:send' }. whenever --update-crontab regenerates the system crontab. For Kamal deployments: cron entries run on the host or in a dedicated cron container. The modern alternative: Solid Queue with recurring tasks (no system cron needed). The debugging pattern: WHENEVER_ENVIRONMENT=production whenever --print-schedule shows what the generated crontab will be. Log output: cron jobs redirect stdout/stderr to log files. Silent cron failure mode: the job runs but produces an error that nobody sees — always redirect to a log file and monitor.

FAILURE: Not using Rails' built-in CSRF protection for JSON API endpoints incorrectly, either over-protecting or under-protecting. UNSAID: for APIs that only serve JSON to JavaScript frontends (SPA or mobile): protect_from_forgery with: :null_session allows the request and returns an empty session on CSRF token mismatch (doesn't raise). For pure API controllers: skip_before_action :verify_authenticity_token and use token-based auth (JWT, API keys) instead. The dangerous middle: a controller that renders both HTML and JSON with token protection disabled — the HTML forms become CSRF vulnerable. The rule: HTML controllers use CSRF protection; API controllers use token-based auth with CSRF disabled; never mix the two patterns in the same controller.

Rails with the Lograge gem provides structured, single-line log output for production, replacing Rails' multi-line default. UNSAID: Default Rails logging produces 5-8 lines per request (Started GET, Processing, SQL queries, Rendered, Completed). Log aggregation tools (Datadog, Splunk, Papertrail) work better with structured single-line JSON logs. Lograge: config.lograge.enabled = true; config.lograge.formatter = Lograge::Formatters::Json.new collapses each request to one JSON line with: method, path, status, duration, db_time, view_time. Custom fields: config.lograge.custom_options = lambda { |event| { user_id: event.payload[:user_id] } }. The structured log enables: filtering by status code, aggregating p99 response times, and tracing slow requests across all services.

Rails with Tailwind UI or TailwindCSS components from Flowbite provides production-ready UI without a custom design system. UNSAID: Tailwind UI (commercial, from Tailwind Labs) provides 500+ copy-paste HTML components with Alpine.js or vanilla JS interactivity. Flowbite provides free Tailwind components. The Rails integration: copy the HTML into ERB partials or ViewComponents, replace placeholder content with Rails helpers and variables. The productivity pattern: prototype with Tailwind UI components, refine with custom CSS, extract into ViewComponents for reuse. The licensing note: Tailwind UI requires a per-developer license for commercial use; Flowbite is MIT licensed.

FAILURE: Running database migrations on every deploy without a deployment strategy causes downtime with long-running migrations. UNSAID: A migration that adds a NOT NULL column without a default on a million-row table: ALTER TABLE posts ADD COLUMN status varchar NOT NULL causes an exclusive table lock for minutes (or hours). During this lock, all reads and writes to posts fail. The zero-downtime migration strategy: 1) Add column as nullable with no lock; 2) Add a default in application code (use new column if present); 3) Backfill in batches (update_all in chunks); 4) Add NOT NULL constraint without lock (SET NOT NULL using PostgreSQL's constraint validation is lock-free after backfill). The Strong Migrations gem enforces these patterns automatically.

Rails with PostgreSQL's LISTEN/NOTIFY enables lightweight pub-sub messaging without Redis or Action Cable for background processes. UNSAID: pg_notify('user_updated', user_id.to_s) sends a notification; ActiveRecord::Base.connection.execute('LISTEN user_updated') receives it. The pg gem's async_notify method blocks waiting for notifications. Use case: database triggers that notify application processes of data changes without polling. Implementing a message broker inside the database: workers LISTEN on channels, producers pg_notify with payloads. The constraint: NOTIFY payloads are limited to 8000 bytes; for larger data, notify with just the record ID and have the listener fetch the full record. This pattern is simpler than Redis pub/sub for apps already on PostgreSQL.

Rails' decision to merge Turbo and Hotwire into the default stack in Rails 7 reflects the framework's response to the React dominance, betting on server-rendered HTML + minimal JS as a productivity-equivalent alternative. UNSAID: React solved real problems (dynamic UIs, component reuse) but introduced SPAs with: separate frontend/backend codebases, build tools, npm ecosystem complexity, and hydration overhead. Turbo + Hotwire re-captures the Rails 'convention over configuration' philosophy for UIs: 90% of SPA patterns (navigation without reload, partial updates, real-time data) are achievable with Turbo's 3KB JavaScript. For the remaining 10% (complex client-side state, offline support, native animations): React remains superior. The Rails bet: the 90% case is what most business apps need.

Rails with ActionText and custom attachment renders enables rich embeds (tweets, videos, code blocks) in user-generated content. UNSAID: Action Text's attachable protocol: any model implementing attachable (defining to_trix_content_attachment_partial_path) can be embedded in Trix editor content. Override ActionText::Attachable to render custom HTML for embedded records. Custom embed example: a CodeBlock model embedded in a post renders as a syntax-highlighted block. The media embed pattern: a URL pasted into Trix triggers an oEmbed lookup that wraps the URL in a rich embed attachment. This enables Medium-style rich embeds within Rails' standard content pipeline without custom JavaScript editors.

FAILURE: Using Rails' redirect_to with user-controlled input without validation enables open redirect attacks. UNSAID: redirect_to params[:return_to] — a phishing attacker sends a link to your site with return_to=https://evil.com. After login, the user is redirected to the attacker's site. The fix: validate that the redirect URL is same-origin: url = URI.parse(params[:return_to]); redirect_to(url.host.nil? ? params[:return_to] : root_path). Rails' allow_other_host: true is required for intentional cross-origin redirects (rare). The Brakeman scanner detects this pattern and flags it as a high-severity finding. All login, logout, and post-action return_to redirects must validate the target URL.

Rails with Active Record's insert_all and upsert_all handles bulk data operations efficiently in Rails 6+. UNSAID: Post.insert_all([{title: 'A', content: '...'}, {title: 'B', content: '...'}]) inserts multiple records in a single SQL INSERT statement, skipping duplicates based on the primary key. Post.upsert_all([...]) inserts or updates. The performance: 1000 record insert = 1 query vs 1000 queries with create(). Constraints: insert_all skips callbacks, validations, and timestamps (updated_at is not set). For bulk imports: use insert_all with explicit timestamps: Post.insert_all(records.map { |r| r.merge(created_at: Time.current, updated_at: Time.current) }). The returning option specifies columns to return from inserted rows for further processing.

Rails with Active Record's strict_loading prevents accidental N+1 queries by raising an error when a lazy-loaded association is accessed. UNSAID: User.strict_loading.find(id) raises ActiveRecord::StrictLoadingViolationError when post.comments is accessed without eager loading. config.active_record.strict_loading_by_default = true in production config enforces this globally. In development: helps catch N+1s during feature development before they hit production. The configuration: strict_loading: :n_plus_one_only (default in some configs) only raises on associations that would trigger N+1, not on all lazy loading. For models: has_many :comments, strict_loading: true enforces eager loading for specific associations.

Rails with PostgreSQL's advisory locks provides application-level distributed locking without a lock table. UNSAID: ApplicationRecord.connection.execute('SELECT pg_advisory_lock(123)') acquires a session-level advisory lock — no other process with the same lock ID can proceed until the first releases it. Use case: ensuring a background job runs only once across multiple workers; preventing concurrent processing of the same resource. The pattern: use a deterministic integer derived from the resource ID (Zlib.crc32("resource:#{id}")). pg_try_advisory_lock is non-blocking: returns false immediately if lock is held, enabling try-and-skip patterns. Session locks are released when the database connection closes; transaction locks release on COMMIT/ROLLBACK.

Rails 8 complete production stack represents the framework's most opinionated and self-contained configuration in its 20-year history. UNSAID: The Rails 8 production stack: Ruby 3.3+ (YJIT enabled by default for 15-25% performance improvement), Puma (multi-threaded server), Solid Queue + Solid Cache + Solid Cable (database-backed trio replacing Redis), Propshaft (simple assets), Kamal 2 (Docker deployment to any VPS), Thruster (HTTP/2, SSL, asset caching proxy), Authentication Generator (session-based auth), Brakeman (security scanning), RuboCop Rails Omakase (style). This stack is the DHH-vision of a one-person framework: deploy a production Rails 8 app with a single kamal setup && kamal deploy, no PaaS required.

FAILURE: Using Rails' send or public_send with user-controlled method names enables arbitrary method invocation. UNSAID: user.send(params[:action]) allows calling any method on the user object including destroy, update_all, or any system method. This is a direct code injection vulnerability. The fix: always use an allowlist: ALLOWED_ACTIONS = %w[activate deactivate]; user.public_send(params[:action]) if ALLOWED_ACTIONS.include?(params[:action]). Even public_send without allowlist is dangerous — it restricts to public methods but still allows calling disruptive public methods. Brakeman detects dynamic dispatch patterns and flags them for review. Never use send with user input.

Rails with the Scenic gem and PostgreSQL database views enables analytics and reporting queries as first-class ActiveRecord models. UNSAID: create_view :user_statistics, sql_definition: <<-SQL replaces the complex JOIN query with a model: UserStatistic.find(user_id). The view definition is stored in db/views/user_statistics_v01.sql (versioned). Querying: UserStatistic.where(active: true) generates SELECT * FROM user_statistics WHERE active = true — the database executes the view definition. For materialized views: refresh on a schedule or on data change. The reporting use case: user_statistics materializes counts, sums, and aggregates that would be expensive to compute on demand. Scenic enables treating complex reporting queries as standard Rails models.

Ruby's object-oriented purity and blocks/closures make Rails' DSL design patterns possible in ways other languages can't match as elegantly. UNSAID: Ruby's ability to accept blocks, use method_missing, open classes, and evaluate code in object context enables the Rails DSL: has_many :posts do; def recent; ... end; end extends the association with custom methods defined in a block. The enum DSL, validation macros, callback macros, and routing DSL are all Ruby objects responding to class-level method calls. This is why Rails ports to other languages (Sails.js, Grails) feel heavier — JavaScript and Java's syntax doesn't support the same seamless DSL construction. Ruby's expressiveness is a genuine competitive advantage for Rails' productivity claims, not just aesthetics.
